topic: I installed 14 AI tools in a quarter and my deep work collapsed
strategic_angle: Mistake + Workflow Reveal. The tension: senior operators have been promised that 'more AI' is the path to focus, and have quietly experienced the opposite. The carousel resolves the tension by reframing the problem — the bottleneck isn't which AI tools, it's the chat-shaped interaction model itself. Lumen is named only in slides 7-8, after the audience has already adopted the new frame.

slides.headline: I installed 14 AI tools last quarter. My deep work collapsed.
slides.visual_concept: A wooden desk in a quiet study at dusk, a glass dome floating above it, fractured from the inside as if pressure escaped outward; warm amber rim light from upper left, deep shadows, atmospheric haze.
slides.image_prompt: A still-life scene at dusk in a quiet study. Centered above a small wooden desk floats a glass dome, fractured from the inside outward — as if internal pressure cracked the glass. Single warm rim light from the upper-left at ~3200K, cool ambient fill from below, atmospheric haze with light dust motes catching the warm light. Hyperreal materials: glass with light-absorption properties, wood with visible grain. The dome occupies the upper two-thirds of the frame; the bottom-third is intentionally empty for headline placement. Cinematic 35mm photograph, shallow depth of field, deep blacks held to #0E0F12. Composition leaves room at the bottom-left for typography. No text, no logos, no watermark, no real brand names, no flat 2D illustration look, no AI-typical hand anatomy, no plastic skin, no faces, no neon glow, no dashboard mockups.

Slide number 01/08 top-right in IBM Plex Mono. Brand mark 'lumen' bottom-right at 14px in #F4F1EC.

caption:
I installed fourteen 'AI productivity' tools last quarter. By the end of it, my deep-work time had been cut in half.

The tools weren't the problem. The interaction model was.

Every chat box is an interruption with a friendly face. You go in to do one thing, you come out three tabs deeper, and your morning has dissolved.

Senior operators aren't more disciplined. They have fewer surfaces where they can be interrupted. The advantage is structural, not behavioral.

What I removed first, in order:

1. Anything that lived in a chat sidebar of an existing tool.
2. Anything that pinged me before I'd finished my first read of the day.
3. Anything that asked me to 'configure' rather than just running with sensible defaults.

What replaced them is a system that gives me one read at 8am, one read at 5pm, and nothing in between.

If your AI stack has gotten louder than your work, it's probably not the model. It's the interface.

Tell me — what's the one tool you'd kill in your stack tomorrow if you could?

— Mira
cta: Save this for the next time you're about to install another AI tool.
first_comment: If you want the three questions I now ask before installing anything new — short version: Does it interrupt me? Does it require configuration? Does it want my morning? — they're a single page on lumen.example/three-questions. Genuinely no signup; we made the page after this many DMs asked for it.
alternative_hooks: I installed 14 AI tools last quarter. My deep work collapsed.
Most 'AI productivity' tools quietly destroy your deep work.
Senior operators don't have more focus. They have fewer surfaces.
Stop installing AI tools that interrupt you.
Three categories of AI tools to remove from your week. In order.
